EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed northeast from the Tennessee Valley and moved along and off the coast. An accumulating snow took place across interior portions of Northeastern New Jersey, with far northwestern sections towards Western Passaic, Western Essex, and Northern Bergen receiving an accumulating snow of 3 to 6 inches. Further south and east where the snow mixed with, and even changed to rain amounts were much lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow took place across the zone, with amounts that were reported between 3 and 5 inches. Cocorahs reports were 4.5 inches in Wayne, 4.3 inches in West Milfored, 4.1 inches in Little Falls, and a trained spotter came in also with 3.8 inches in Wayne. These amounts fell short of warning criteria with the snow going over to mixed precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed northeast from the Tennessee Valley and moved along and off the coast. An accumulating snow took place across interior portions of Northeastern New Jersey, with far northwestern sections towards Western Passaic, Western Essex, and Northern Bergen receiving an accumulating snow of 3 to 6 inches. Further south and east where the snow mixed with, and even changed to rain amounts were much lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulating snow took place before a mix and change to rain. Reports averaged in the 2 to 4.5 inch range for the most part. Cocorahs reported 4.0 inches in Verona and 3.5 inches at 1.3 NE West Caldwell.  There was a public report of 4.4 inches in North Caldwell and 3.5 inches in West Orange. Amounts fell short of warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved northeast from the Mid-Atlantic Region to southern New England during the night of January 9, 2024. Its center passed just north of the region with the pressure gradient tightening as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Northeastern New Jersey during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1115 PM Local Standard Time, the Department of Highways reported in Fort Lee, NJ a downed tree on Routes 1 & 9 northbound, north of Bergen Boulevard with all lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed northeast from the Tennessee Valley and moved along and off the coast. An accumulating snow took place across interior portions of Northeastern New Jersey, with far northwestern sections towards Western Passaic, Western Essex, and Northern Bergen receiving an accumulating snow of 3 to 6 inches. Further south and east where the snow mixed with, and even changed to rain amounts were much lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulating snow took place before a change to a wintry mix. Trained spotters reported 6.2 inches at 2 NNE Franklin Lakes, 5.7 inches at 1 N Franklin Lakes, 4.0 inches at Franklin Lakes, 4.0 inches at Glen Rock, and 4.0 inches at 1 ESE Westwood, and 3.4 inches in Fair Lawn. A Cocorahs observer reported 5.5 inches at Oakland.  Amounts fell off quickly heading southeast. Amounts averaged below warning criteria across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded low pressure system brought heavy rain on the front end, with a change to heavy wet accumulating snow in higher elevations as the upper level low approached. Their were much lower amounts in lower elevations. The upper low swung through on the back end of the system early on the 22nd resulted in height falls and colder air working in changing the precipitation to mainly wet snow across portions of the interior, especially in elevated locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 7.5 inches at 5 N West Milford and a cocorahs observer reported 3.4 inches in West Milford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Monmouth County. Water entered vulnerable buildings causing some damage. The tide gauge at Keansburg reached 8.02 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Sandy Hook reached 7.84 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Sea Bright reached 6.96 feet MLLW, the tide gauge at Belmar reached 7.24 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Manasquan reached 7.05 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary just south of the region resulted in stagnant, moist air across the area. Warm air lifted over the boundary facilitated the development of dense fog late on the 26th. The fog dissipated early on the 27th as the front shifted south and drier air advected into the area from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was observed for several hours at Philadelphia International Airport, Northeast Philadelphia Airport, South Jersey Regional Airport and Atlantic City International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked to the northwest of the forecast area on Tuesday into early Wednesday (January 9-10th). A cold front associated with the low swept through the region on Wednesday. In the system���s wake, total rainfall amounts varied from about 1.50 inches up to about 4.00 inches. The rain fell on already saturated soils and there was some snow on the ground that melted and also contributed to the runoff.  As a result, river flooding was prevalent across the Passaic Basin, Raritan Basin, and southeast Pennsylvania basins including the Schuylkill and Brandywine. The Brandywine flooding extended in to Delaware. Smaller streams and rivers across northern New Castle County in Delaware, such as the Christina, Red Clay, and White Clay also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across the Passaic Basin, flooding occurred along the Pequannock River. Macopin crested at 6.1 ft.  The Wanaque River at Wanaque flooded, cresting at 7.36 ft. Flooding occurred along the main-stem Passaic River with Chatham cresting at 6.85 ft, Pine Brook at 21.04 ft, Singac at 11.95 ft, Millington at 8.30 ft, and Little Falls at 9.71 ft. These all exceeded their flood stages. Flooding occurred along the Rockaway River. Boonton below the dam crested at 7.82 ft. The Ramapo River at Pompton Lakes flooded crested at 13.79 ft. The Pompton River also flooded at Pompton Plains crested at 20.38 ft. Again, all of these locations exceeded their flood stages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was observed for several hours at South Jersey Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds resulted in widespread tidal flooding for much of the coast line and tidal waterways. The outcome was the tidal inundation of roads and structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system over the area on August 18th produced heavy thunderstorms that were fairly slow moving over northeast New Jersey. A cold front moving in from the west late in the afternoon produced heavy thunderstorms with an environmental MUCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and PWAT values of 1.7-1.8 inches. These slow moving thunderstorms produce rainfall rates of 1-3 inches per hour over New Jersey with total rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches. This resulted in widespread flash flooding during the evening of August 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Green Brook at Seeley Mills reach flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure shifted northeast through the eastern Great Lakes on January 13, 2024, sending a warm front through during the morning hours, followed quickly by a cold front. Strong winds produced by this system, combined with peaking high astronomical tides associated with a recent new moon, produced widespread moderate coastal flooding along the tidal waterways of Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred at the Bergen Point NY NOS tidal gauge, with water levels above the moderate threshold of 8.5 ft MLLW from 924 AM EST to 1106 AM EST, and a peak water level of 9.0 ft MLLW from 1000 AM EST to 1030 AM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred at the Bergen Point NY NOS tidal gauge, with water levels above the moderate threshold of 8.5 ft MLLW from 924 AM EST to 1106 AM EST, and a peak water level of 9.0 ft MLLW from 1000 AM EST to 1030 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light snowfall accumulations across the region, with a narrow mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurring near and just south of I-78. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band only about 10 to 15 miles wide or less from Berks County, PA eastward toward Raritan Bay in New Jersey with totals near a foot. A coating of snow also occurred well north and south of the band with amounts around 2 to 4. While total snowfall amounts within the band were rather impressive, generally falling within about 3-4 hours, impacts were limited as roadway temperatures hovered near freezing and the snowfall occurred late on a Friday night and early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 2 to 5 across the zone, however the extreme northern portion of the county near Raritan Bay likely received near 6 to 10 based on nearby reports. The highest snowfall report was from Long Branch with 5.0.

While total snowfall amounts within the band were rather impressive, generally falling within about 3-4 hours, impacts were limited as roadway temperatures hovered near freezing and the snowfall occurred late on a Friday night and early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 2 to 4 across the county. The highest snowfall report was from Monroe Township with 5.0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy thunderstorms developed over the area on the afternoon of August 6th. A slow moving frontal system stalled out over the area. PWATs of 2-2.2 inches and a sharp gradient of MUCAPE peaking out over the area near 2500 J/kg allowed for the development of strong thunderstorms producing torrential rainfall with rates upwards of 1.5-2.5 inches per hour. Total rainfall was 1-3 inches with localized amount up to 4-5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on US 46 Eastbound at CR 628 and Piaget Ave with all lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system over the area on August 18th produced heavy thunderstorms that were fairly slow moving over northeast New Jersey. A cold front moving in from the west late in the afternoon produced heavy thunderstorms with an environmental MUCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and PWAT values of 1.7-1.8 inches. These slow moving thunderstorms produce rainfall rates of 1-3 inches per hour over New Jersey with total rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches. This resulted in widespread flash flooding during the evening of August 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars stuck in flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough slowly approached the area as a stalled frontal boundary sat just to our west. With an environment with about 1500 J/kg of MLCAPE and 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear, a thunderstorm that developed was able to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Milford police dispatch reported a few trees down around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to major flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries with both high tides on January 10th. There were road closures with the flood waters approaching and entering some vulnerable buildings.  The tide gauge at Newbold, Pennsylvania reached a level of 12.64 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to major flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries with both high tides on January 10th. There were road closures with the flood waters approaching and entering some vulnerable buildings.  The tide gauge at Newbold, Pennsylvania reached a level of 12.64 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at Burlington reached a record level of 11.99 feet MLLW. Multiple homes in Delran were condemned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary just south of the region resulted in stagnant, moist air across the area. Warm air lifted over the boundary facilitated the development of dense fog late on the 26th. The fog dissipated early on the 27th as the front shifted south and drier air advected into the area from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was observed for several hours at Wilmington Airport and Millville Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal surface low moved north/northeastward on Wednesday night into Thursday (December 27-28th). Total rainfall amounts across the forecast area varied from about 1.00 inches up to 3.00 inches. The heaviest rains fell west of the I-95 corridor. This heavy rain fell on already saturated soils. As a result, river flooding was prevalent across the Passaic Basin, Raritan Basin, and southeast Pennsylvania basins including the Schuylkill and Brandywine. The Brandywine flooding extended in to Delaware. Smaller streams and rivers across northern New Castle County in Delaware, such as the Christina, Red Clay, and White Clay also flooded. ||The flooding along the Passaic River crossed months and persisted into January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across the Passaic Basin, flooding occurred along the Wanaque River. The Wanaque gauge crested at 6.02 ft. Flooding occurred along the main-stem Passaic River with Pine Brook cresting at 19.86 ft, and Little Falls at 7.47 ft. These all exceeded their flood stages. Flooding also occurred along the Rockaway River. Boonton below the dam crested at 6.03 ft, again, exceeding its flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance associated with a confluent upper level trough lead to strong mid level frontogenesis that developed a band of snow to the north of developing low pressure moving offshore. Southern portions of the area were on the northern edge of a narrow band of moderate to heavy snow.  Amounts were considerably less to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far northern edge of a band of accumulating snow got into Western Essex County with a general 2 to 4 inches reported from north to south. A trained spotter reported 2.0 inches further north into West Caldwell, while the general public reported 3.0 inches in West Orange, and 4.0 inches further south into Millburn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure both at the surface and aloft combined with a flow of moist air northward from the Gulf of Mexico resulted in excessive heat across parts of central and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index reached 105��F at Somerset Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow got into Western Union County, with the highest amounts over the southern half of the zone. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern edge of a narrow band of heavy snow got into Eastern Union County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light snowfall accumulations across the region, with a narrow mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurring near and just south of I-78. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band only about 10 to 15 miles wide or less from Berks County, PA eastward toward Raritan Bay in New Jersey with totals near a foot. A coating of snow also occurred well north and south of the band with amounts around 2 to 4. While total snowfall amounts within the band were rather impressive, generally falling within about 3-4 hours, impacts were limited as roadway temperatures hovered near freezing and the snowfall occurred late on a Friday night and early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 8 to 12 across the county along a narrow 10 to 15 mile wide band, although only 3 to 6 occurred in the northern third of the county mainly near and north of I-78. The highest snowfall report was from Alexandria Township with 14.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Tennessee Valley and the Gulf states tracked tracked north, and redeveloped and intensified rapidly off the Mid Atlantic coast. A wet snowfall occurred across Northeastern New Jersey, with heavy wet snow across northern and interior portions of Northeast New Jersey, and lesser amounts across southeastern portions.  Marginal surface temperatures precluded higher snow amounts in urban locations and further southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow accumulated across Western Passaic County. A trained spotter at 1 NE West Milford reported 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the area during the evening of February 28th, 2024 and brought high winds to Northeastern New Jersey behind its passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 66 MPH was recorded at the Robbins Reef mesonet site in Bayonne, NJ at 848 PM Local Standard Time. The elevation of this measurement is 69 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light to heavy snowfall accumulations across the region. The heaviest snowfall occurred across portions of northern New Jersey with totals around a foot. A coating of snow also occurred as far south as Camden, Burlington, and Ocean Counties with amounts around 1 to 2. While total snowfall amounts were less than impressive in the central and southern portions of the state, heavy wet snow falling around the time of the morning rush hour resulted in slippery roadways and numerous traffic incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 9 to 14 across much of the county. The highest snowfall report was from Sussex borough with 15.0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper-level trough approached from the west along with associated surface cold front and pre-frontal trough. The pre-frontal trough moved into an area with high instability (1750-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE). This combined with Effect Bulk Shear values around 25 kt allowed for multiple strong to severe thunderstorms to develop. One storm was able to produce damaging wind gusts across northeast NJ, with DCAPE values around 750-1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires down on White Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and trough produced thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening on July 17th that resulted in localized flash flooding in northern New Jersey. MUCAPE values of 1000-2000 J/kg and PWATs of 2-2.2 inches allowed the thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall rates of 1-2 per hour. The thunderstorms produced rainfall totals of 1 to 2.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood rescues occurring on West Pleasant Street and Green Street in Hackensack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a warm and moist airmass, with several strong to severe thunderstorms developing. Strong downbursts were reported across portions of New Jersey, resulting in several trees down. A landspout also occurred in central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 strength landspout tornado occurred in Lawrence Township in Mercer County. The landspout began near the Marwa Service Center and Conoco gas station near the intersection of Johnson Road and US Route 1 Business (Brunswick Avenue), where a few trees were uprooted and some softwood trees were snapped. A couple trees|fell on the service station, causing some collateral damage. The gas station canopy was not damaged, but a large sign behind the building was snapped. Two large clothes donation bins were tossed into a brushy area. Surveillance footage from the service center showed a distinct convergent nature and shift to the winds as the damage occurred.||The landspout then moved northeast into a small wooded area before emerging into a post office parking lot. About a half dozen vehicles were flipped over in the parking lot, and a few parking lot signs were blown over or lofted and carried about 250 yards |away across the highway. The post office building sustained some minor structural damage, including uplift of some metal roofing material. An HVAC unit was blown off of the roof of the building. A large tree was uprooted at the front of the building and a few |other trees had several limbs blown down. Cell phone footage taken from across the highway at the Auto Lenders property showed a distinct circulation as the damage was occurring, lofting debris vertically. The landspout then dissipated after impacting the post office property and no additional damage was observed downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow with a potent storm moving up the NJ/Delaware coast resulted in water piling up along the New Jersey coast and within the back bays. Water also moved up the Delaware Bay, which also resulted in tidal flooding within the Delaware River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approached aloft as a cold front approached at the surface. Clusters of showers and thunderstorms developed west of the area. Given 0-6 km Bulk Shear and MLCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, one storm became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low hanging wires over Eagle Rock Road in Roseland due to thunderstorm wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning precipitation event with warm advection resulting in wintry precipitation near and north of the I-78 corridor in eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Wet snow changed to rain, including some light freezing rain and ice accretion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around 1-3 across much of the county, with maximum values near 4. 4.2 was reported in Wantage Twp. Additionally, a light glaze of freezing rain occurred in the higher elevations, as temperatures remained near freezing as precipitation changed to rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from around a dusting to 1 at lower elevations to 2-3 across the higher elevations. 3.0 was reported in Hardwick Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation falling into a cold airmass resulted in some light wintry precipitation accumulations of a light glaze of freezing rain, sleet, and snow from around portions of northern Delmarva, the Philly metro, and northeast into northern New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania. Much of the light icing remained within the New Jersey portions of the Philly metro and northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of sleet, flurries, and light freezing rain lasted for about 1-2 hours. A dusting of snow and sleet accumulation and and a light glaze of ice occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system over the area on August 18th produced heavy thunderstorms that were fairly slow moving over northeast New Jersey. A cold front moving in from the west late in the afternoon produced heavy thunderstorms with an environmental MUCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and PWAT values of 1.7-1.8 inches. These slow moving thunderstorms produce rainfall rates of 1-3 inches per hour over New Jersey with total rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches. This resulted in widespread flash flooding during the evening of August 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paterson Plank Road impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass was in place as a cold front moved in. This resulted in several strong to severe thunderstorms developing, with storms mainly bringing damaging wind gusts to portions of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NJ DOT - STMC: Downed wires on I-287 both directions South of Exit 30 - North Maple Ave (Bernards Twp) All lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and pre-frontal trough moved into a very hot, humid, and unstable airmass, which sparked off several severe thunderstorms, mainly producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down, lines down and a picture of a gazebo collapsed in Delanco. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive upper / mid level trough lead to low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley with a secondary low developing just off the coast. Temperatures were warm and above freezing much of the time along the coast, but often at or below freezing for mainly the northwestern interior. This led to an advisory level snowfall across a good portion of Northeast New Jersey zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer reported 3.0 inches in Harrison and a Cocorahs observer reported 2.9 inches at 1 WSW Hoboken.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cocorahs observer reported 4.0 inches in New Providence and 3.3 inches in Westfield while a trained spotter reported 2. 8 inches in Plainfield.

This led to an advisory level snowfall across a good portion of Northeast New Jersey zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 4.0 inches in West Orange and a Cocorahs observer reported 2.6 inches in Maplewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a warm and moist airmass, with several strong to severe thunderstorms developing. Strong downbursts were reported across portions of New Jersey, resulting in several trees down. A landspout also occurred in central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in area of Jackson. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass brought ripe conditions for the development of thunderstorms as a weak front moved in. Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts bringing downed trees and wires to portions of northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph thunderstorm wind gust measured at the Sandy Hook NOS weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snowfall to portions of eastern Pennsylvania and adjacent areas of northwest New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a dusting to 3 fell at lower elevations across the county, with as much as 6.6 at higher elevations, as reported in Green Pond. Higher elevations ranged from around 4-6, but coverage was less than half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides associated with the full moon and a light onshore flow brought some moderate tidal flooding to the New Jersey coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of Monmouth County. Water entered vulnerable buildings causing some damage. The tide gauge at Belmar reached 6.92 feet MLLW and the tide gauge at Sea Bright reached 6.20 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snowfall to portions of eastern Pennsylvania and adjacent areas of northwest New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from around 1-3 at lower elevations to as much as 20.0 inches, as reported at High Point Monument. Other elevations above about 1,200 feet received between 6-12 of snowfall, including 12.0 at Highland Lakes.
